headlines.title: ELECTRICIANS NOW WILLING TO ACCEPT COMPROMISE SCALE
headlines.fullText: Union electricians" locked out Dec. 31 by Fort Worth contractors are willing to compromise differences and go back to work. The lockout came at the expiration of the electricians' contract, and when they asked for an increase of do cents per day. The present scale calls for $4.50 per day. According to C. W. Woodman, grievance committee chairman, the electricians and the committee representing the contractors have settled all differences except the increase and the electricians have submitted a compromise. "The electricians will go back to work and finish all pending contracts and do other things to bring about a just settlement," said Woodman Tuesday, "but they want an agreement on the wage scale. If the contractors fail to agree and put nonunion men on the jobs, there is no telling what sort of complications may arise. Fort Worth is a good union town and many of its institutions are made possible through organized labor, and any trouble that might bring about the quitting of the unions from the city would be very detrimental. "If nonunion men should be supplemented for union men it would mean the reduction of the number employed and many institutions would have to close and other institutions would be forced out of business."

headlines.title: Cops Cavort in 'Vodeville' Before Big Byers Audience
headlines.subtitle: Skyscraper Traffic Policeman Recites Biography - Mounted Officer Does Blackface Sketch-"The Finest" Smile on Friends.
headlines.fullText: Mirth and jollity reigned Monday night at the Byers theater where an audience that packed the theater witnessed the much heralded "police vodeville," held for the benefit of the Fort Worth Police Benevolent Association. Aided by W. E. Patton, former Orpheum star, who appeared in his famous characterization of the kindly country physician who is by no means so "slow" as his drawling speech would indicate, the entertainment was carried through with vim and dash that so seemed to be the universal verdict of the audience as it filed out of the theater at the close of the performance. Unique features, promised by Police Commissioner Davis, were not lacking. The "finest" lined up in the lobby of the theater and greeted the audience as it entered the door. Patrolmen W. A. Fielder, W. M. Lawrence, Lee Reynolds, A. W. Burford, Ed Black and G. F. Coffey. The first "number" on the program was a speech by Commissioner Davis. The commissioner told of the purpose of the entertainment. He described the perils of a policeman's life and emphasized the fact that there is no pension fund provided by the city for sick or disabled policemen. George Washington Montgomery, the seven-foot traffic cop at Main and Seventh-Montgomery-was there in a "spike-tailed," brass-buttoned blue uniform, told humorously of his early life. Montgomery said he was born at Tulsa, Okla., and that he was 16 years old before he could find trousers that covered his long legs. He told of his experiences traveling as a "South African Wild Congo Carnival Giant," with Miller Brothers' "101 Ranch Wild West Show." Montgomery's "spell" was one of the features of the benefit. Between the second and third acts Patrolman George Cooper appeared in a black-face monologue that kept his auditors in roars of laughter. His make-up was beyond criticism and his "darky dialect" absolutely true to life. Approximately 3,000 tickets were sold, it is estimated. The committee in charge of the entertainment was J. D. Allgood, chairman; Charles Page, secretary-treasurer; George Cooper, Ed Black and Charles Traxler. Some of the credit for the success of the benefit is due to patrolmen who worked early and late to forward the ticket selling campaign.

headlines.title: WILSON WILL FINISH VACATION ON SUNDAY
headlines.subtitle: President Abandons Plan to Visit New Orleans Because of Rush of Work.
headlines.fullText: By Associated Press. PASS CHRISTIAN, Miss., Jan. 6 .- President Wilson has definitely abandoned his plans to make an informal visit to New Orleans before his vacation in the south is ended. In spite of the fact that every effort was made to sift out everything but flatters of the most pressing importance, the deluge from the first mail received in a day here and Friday Mr. Wilson found himself compelled to attend to many of them. The President is working on a number of important matters here and has found it necessary to make careful use of the remainder of his time before returning to Washington. Whether or not Mr. Wilson has sketched his message dealing with the trusts and "big business" is not known and the President has not mentioned it. It is now planned for the presidential party to leave here late Sunday night, returning to the capital next Tuesday morning. As there is a diplomatic dinner at the White House on Tuesday night there is no chance that the President's vacation will be prolonged. The President was as usual and proved him to be in better health than he has enjoyed for months.

headlines.title: MRS. BOOTH TO VISIT TEXAS PENITENTIARIES
headlines.subtitle: Governor and Wife Will Accompany Noted Prison Worker on Tour of State.
headlines.fullText: AUSTIN, Jan. 6 .- Accompanied by Governor and Mrs. Colquitt, Mrs. Maude Ballington Booth of New York, volunteer reformer and Head of the Volunteer Prison League of America, will make a thorough inspection of the Texas penitentiaries. The governor some time ago extended an invitation to Mrs. Booth to visit the state and has received a message in which she advises that she will go to California next month and on her way will stop here and make the inspection. Chairman Murray of the penitentiary system was advised of the coming visit.

headlines.title: GIRLS KILLED, BELIEF
headlines.fullText: SOUTH BEND, Ind., Jan. 6-Two girls, in addition to the bodies of the eight already recovered, are now believed to be dead in the ruins of the building that collapsed here Saturday. The girls have been missing since before the accident and are known to have been acquaintances of Delbert Hibberg, who was killed. Fire which broke out in the ruins has delayed the search for the bodies.

headlines.title: SEATS OR FINE NEW DENISON MOVIE LAW
headlines.subtitle: Council Passes Drastic Regulation for Picture Shows -Hourly Inspection Planned.
headlines.fullText: DENISON, Texas, Jan. 6.-One of the most drastic regulations for moving picture shows in the Southwest was made here last night. The council made it a fineable offense for any person connected with a show to sell a ticket and not provide a seat. A fine of not less than $1 nor more than $25 was provided. The police are to make hourly inspection of shows.
